learning_objectives: Analyze the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act and its implications
Understand constitutional issues in digital privacy
Distinguish between criminal and civil cyber law
readings: Cybersecurity Law by Jeff Kosseff Ch. 1-2
CFAA legislative text and key case law

lecture_notes.introduction: Cybersecurity does not exist in a legal vacuum. The laws governing digital systems shape what defenders can do, what attackers are prosecuted for, and what organizations are required to protect.
key_concepts: Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA): unauthorized access, exceeding authorized access
Fourth Amendment and digital searches: Carpenter v. United States
Electronic Communications Privacy Act (ECPA) and Stored Communications Act
State-level computer crime laws and variations
International jurisdiction challenges in cybercrime
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the Aaron Swartz case and Van Buren v. United States to understand CFAA scope and 'exceeding authorized access' interpretation

review.value: Criminalizes accessing a computer without authorization or exceeding authorized access. Van Buren (2021) narrowed the definition: using authorized credentials for unauthorized purposes may not violate CFAA.
Supreme Court held that accessing historical cell-site location records requires a warrant. Extended Fourth Amendment protections to digital data revealing intimate details of life.

class_schedule.topic: Data Privacy Regulations: GDPR and US Privacy Laws
learning_objectives: Compare GDPR requirements with US federal and state privacy laws
Identify data controller vs data processor obligations
Apply privacy by design principles to system architecture

lecture_notes.introduction: Data privacy regulations define how organizations collect, process, store, and share personal data. Non-compliance carries severe financial penalties and reputational damage.
key_concepts: GDPR principles: lawfulness, purpose limitation, data minimization, accuracy, storage limitation
Data subject rights: access, erasure, portability, objection
CCPA/CPRA: California privacy rights and opt-out mechanisms
Privacy by design and by default
Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a DPIA for a proposed employee monitoring system, identifying privacy risks and mitigation measures

review.value: Up to 4% of annual global revenue or 20 million euros, whichever is higher. Enforcement by national Data Protection Authorities. Extra-territorial scope applies to any organization processing EU residents' data.
Collect only the personal data that is adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary. This is both a legal requirement and a security best practice: data you don't have can't be breached.

learning_objectives: Apply HIPAA security requirements to healthcare systems
Implement PCI-DSS controls for payment processing
Understand SOX requirements for financial reporting systems

lecture_notes.introduction: Beyond general privacy laws, specific industries face additional regulations that mandate security controls. These regulations define minimum security standards with specific technical requirements.
key_concepts: HIPAA Security Rule: administrative, physical, and technical safeguards
PCI-DSS v4.0: 12 requirements for cardholder data protection
SOX Section 404: internal controls for financial reporting
Sector-specific regulations: GLBA (finance), FERPA (education), COPPA (children)
Compliance vs security: meeting minimums vs achieving actual protection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping PCI-DSS v4.0 requirements to technical security controls and identifying gaps in a sample e-commerce architecture

review.value: Administrative: risk analysis, workforce training, contingency plans. Physical: facility access controls, workstation security. Technical: access controls, audit controls, transmission security, integrity controls.
Applies to any system that stores, processes, or transmits cardholder data. Scope reduction through network segmentation and tokenization minimizes compliance burden.

learning_objectives: Determine when a security incident triggers breach notification obligations

lecture_notes.introduction: When a breach occurs, organizations face a web of notification requirements that vary by jurisdiction, type of data, and number of affected individuals. Failure to notify properly can result in penalties exceeding those for the breach itself.
key_concepts: GDPR: 72-hour notification to supervisory authority
US state breach notification laws: 50 states, 50 different requirements
Notification triggers: personal data types, risk of harm assessment
Notification content: what happened, what data, what to do
HHS breach notification for HIPAA covered entities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulating a breach notification response: determining obligations across multiple jurisdictions, drafting notifications, and managing the notification timeline

review.value: GDPR requires notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach. If notification is delayed, the reason for delay must be documented.

Each US state has its own breach notification law with different definitions of personal information, different timeframes, and different notification methods. Organizations must comply with all applicable state laws.

class_schedule.topic: International Cybersecurity Law and Cyber Warfare
learning_objectives: Analyze the Tallinn Manual's application of international law to cyber operations

lecture_notes.introduction: Cyber operations transcend borders, creating complex legal challenges. International law is still evolving to address state-sponsored attacks, cyber espionage, and cross-border criminal activity.
key_concepts: Tallinn Manual: applying law of armed conflict to cyber operations
Budapest Convention: international cybercrime treaty
Mutual Legal Assistance Treaties (MLATs) for cross-border evidence
State sovereignty in cyberspace
Attribution challenges and proportional response
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the NotPetya attack through the lens of international law: attribution, state responsibility, and the application of Tallinn Manual rules

review.value: Non-binding academic study applying existing international law to cyber operations. Rule 80: cyber operations that cause physical damage or injury constitute a 'use of force' under UN Charter Article 2(4).

International law requires attribution before response. Cyber attribution is technically difficult and politically contentious. States may have high-confidence technical attribution but choose not to reveal sources and methods.

class_schedule.topic: Emerging Legal Issues: AI, Encryption, and Liability
learning_objectives: Evaluate legal implications of AI in cybersecurity decision-making
Analyze the encryption debate between privacy and law enforcement
Assess evolving liability frameworks for software vulnerabilities

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology evolves faster than law. Emerging issues around AI, encryption policy, and software liability will shape the cybersecurity legal landscape for decades.
key_concepts: AI in security: liability for false positives/negatives, bias in ML detection
EU AI Act: risk-based regulation of AI systems
Encryption policy: backdoor mandates vs privacy rights
Software liability: negligence, strict liability, safe harbor
Cyber insurance: coverage scope, exclusions, market evolution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Debating the encryption backdoor question: students argue for law enforcement access vs privacy advocates, applying legal frameworks to support positions

review.value: Law enforcement argues encryption prevents access to evidence. Privacy advocates argue backdoors weaken security for everyone. No technical solution provides access only to legitimate authorities without creating exploitable weakness.
Currently, software vendors disclaim most liability through EULAs. Emerging policy (EU Cyber Resilience Act) shifts liability to manufacturers for insecure defaults and unpatched vulnerabilities.

quizzes.topic: CFAA, Privacy Laws, and Compliance Regulations

questions.question: A penetration tester is hired by Company A to test their web application. During testing, the tester discovers that Company A's application is hosted on shared infrastructure, and their SQL injection exploit accidentally accesses Company B's database on the same server. Analyze the legal implications under CFAA and explain what the tester should do.
questions.answer: Under CFAA, the tester accessed Company B's computer without authorization. The Rules of Engagement authorize testing only of Company A's systems. Even though the access was unintentional, CFAA does not require intent for all offenses (18 USC 1030(a)(2) criminalizes intentionally accessing a computer without authorization and obtaining information). The tester should: (1) Immediately stop all interaction with Company B's data. (2) Document exactly what was accessed (for transparency, not evidence tampering). (3) Notify the engagement manager and Company A's legal counsel immediately. (4) Do not view, copy, or analyze any of Company B's data. (5) The incident demonstrates why Rules of Engagement must precisely define scope including infrastructure boundaries, and why shared hosting creates legal risk for penetration testing.

questions.question: A US-based SaaS company with no EU offices processes personal data of EU residents as part of its service. Does GDPR apply? If the company suffers a breach exposing EU resident data, what are the notification obligations and potential penalties?
questions.answer: Yes, GDPR applies extraterritorially under Article 3(2) to any organization that processes personal data of EU residents when offering goods or services to them, regardless of whether the organization has an EU establishment. Notification obligations: (1) Notify the lead supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach (Article 33). Must include: nature of breach, categories and approximate number of data subjects affected, likely consequences, measures taken. (2) Notify affected data subjects without undue delay if the breach is likely to result in high risk to their rights (Article 34). Penalties: Up to 4% of annual global turnover or 20 million euros. The company must designate an EU representative under Article 27.

quizzes.topic: Breach Notification, International Law, and Emerging Issues

questions.question: A healthcare organization suffers a ransomware attack. 50,000 patient records (including SSNs, diagnoses, and treatment data) were exfiltrated before encryption. The organization operates in California, New York, and Texas. List all the regulatory bodies that must be notified, the different timeframes, and the specific notification requirements for each.
questions.answer: Regulatory notifications: (1) HHS/OCR: HIPAA requires notification within 60 days for breaches affecting 500+ individuals. Must also notify prominent media outlets in each affected state. (2) California AG: CCPA/CPRA notification without unreasonable delay. For 500+ CA residents, must also notify the AG. Content must include: what happened, what information was involved, what the organization is doing, what individuals can do. (3) New York AG: NY SHIELD Act requires notification to AG, Department of State, and Division of State Police in the most expedient time possible. (4) Texas AG: Must notify AG within 60 days if 250+ TX residents affected. (5) All affected individuals: each state requires individual notification. California and New York require it without unreasonable delay. Offer credit monitoring services given SSN exposure.

rubric.key: Identifies all applicable regulators (HIPAA, state AGs)

questions.question: A government proposes legislation requiring all messaging apps to provide a 'lawful access' backdoor for encrypted communications when presented with a court order. Argue both sides: (1) Why law enforcement needs this capability, and (2) Why the cybersecurity community opposes it. Conclude with your own analysis.
questions.answer: Law enforcement argument: End-to-end encryption creates 'warrant-proof' spaces where child exploitation, terrorism planning, and organized crime communications are invisible to lawful investigation. Even with a valid court order, law enforcement cannot access the content. This undermines the rule of law and public safety. Cybersecurity opposition: Any backdoor mechanism creates a vulnerability that can be exploited by nation-state adversaries, criminals, and malicious insiders. There is no technical design that provides access only to legitimate authorities. Key escrow systems become high-value targets. Mandating backdoors in one jurisdiction pushes criminals to offshore apps while weakening security for all law-abiding users. Weakened encryption harms human rights activists, journalists, and dissidents globally. Analysis: The technical reality is that a backdoor for one is a backdoor for all. History demonstrates that 'lawful access' mechanisms are inevitably compromised (e.g., Greek wiretapping scandal, Juniper ScreenOS backdoor). Alternative investigative techniques (metadata analysis, endpoint access, informants) should be enhanced instead.

rubric.key: Informed personal analysis with technical reasoning

major_assignments.title: Cybersecurity Legal Compliance Assessment
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive legal and regulatory compliance assessment for a fictional multinational organization. Identify applicable laws, assess compliance gaps, and develop a remediation roadmap.
objectives: Identify all applicable cybersecurity laws and regulations
Assess current compliance posture against requirements
deliverables: Gap analysis report with specific control deficiencies
Remediation roadmap with timeline and cost estimates
Breach notification playbook for multi-jurisdiction response

criteria: Gap severity rated by regulatory risk
Both technical and organizational gaps identified
Prioritized by risk and regulatory penalty
Quick wins identified separately from strategic initiatives

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of cybersecurity law and regulatory knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Case study analyses and compliance exercises

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering CFAA, privacy laws, and compliance regulations

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all cybersecurity law and policy topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions and debates. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: In-class debates are team-based. All written analyses and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Legal analyses must cite primary sources (statutes, case law, regulations). Unattributed copying of legal analysis is plagiarism.
course_policies.professional_conduct: Debates must be conducted professionally. Respect opposing viewpoints. Legal arguments must be based on law and evidence, not personal attacks.
